glossary.def: A plot of true positive rate against false positive rate as the decision threshold sweeps from high to low. Because both axes condition on the true class, the ROC curve is invariant to class prevalence and summarizes a model's discrimination independent of how positives and negatives are mixed.

The area under the ROC curve. By the Wilcoxon-Mann-Whitney identity it equals the probability that a random positive is scored above a random negative (ties split evenly): 1.0 is a perfect ranker, 0.5 is a coin flip, and below 0.5 means the score is reversed.

A plot of precision against recall across thresholds, with no-skill baseline equal to the positive rate. Because precision's denominator includes false positives, the PR curve is acutely sensitive to prevalence and is the more informative summary when positives are rare.

The Kolmogorov-Smirnov statistic in scoring: the maximum gap between the cumulative distributions of the score among positives and negatives, equivalently the largest vertical distance between the ROC curve and the chance diagonal. A standard credit-scoring measure of separation ranging 0 to 1.

AUC rescaled so a random model scores 0 and a perfect model scores 1, via Gini = 2*AUC - 1. It is the ratio of the area between the ROC curve and the diagonal to the area for a perfect model, and is reported alongside KS in banking model documentation.

The property that a predicted probability matches the observed frequency: among cases assigned probability p, a fraction p are truly positive. It is orthogonal to discrimination - a model can rank perfectly yet emit dishonest probabilities - and is inspected with a reliability curve.

The mean squared error of predicted probabilities against binary outcomes, mean((p - y)^2). It is a strictly proper scoring rule (uniquely minimized by the true probabilities) that bundles calibration and refinement, so it is read alongside the reliability curve, never alone.

The decision cutoff that minimizes expected misclassification cost. For calibrated probabilities it has the closed form t* = c_FP / (c_FP + c_FN), depending only on the ratio of error costs; the default 0.5 is correct only when the two error types cost the same.

Hand, D. J. (2009). Measuring Classifier Performance: A Coherent Alternative to the Area Under the ROC Curve. Machine Learning 77(1).
Niculescu-Mizil, A. & Caruana, R. (2005). Predicting Good Probabilities With Supervised Learning. ICML 2005.
Saito, T. & Rehmsmeier, M. (2015). The Precision-Recall Plot Is More Informative than the ROC Plot When Evaluating Binary Classifiers on Imbalanced Datasets. PLOS ONE 10(3).
Brier, G. W. (1950). Verification of Forecasts Expressed in Terms of Probability. Monthly Weather Review 78(1).
Hanley, J. A. & McNeil, B. J. (1982). The Meaning and Use of the Area Under a Receiver Operating Characteristic (ROC) Curve. Radiology 143(1).
Guo, C., Pleiss, G., Sun, Y. & Weinberger, K. Q. (2017). On Calibration of Modern Neural Networks. ICML 2017.
